entries.text: greetings distinguished viewers my name
is Milan and I'm an animation artist
currently employed in the Korean
Animation studio so as you may have
guessed I'm not from round here and my
journey into this industry has
been let's call it a learning curve if
you've ever wondered what it's like to
work in a Korean Animation Studio as a
foreigner whether it's the job hunting
process the War culture or the weird
office Traditions that they do not put
in the brochures then stick around I got
stories and no I should not be divulging
any industry shattering Secrets I do
after all enjoy being employed but um
I'll give you an honest look into what
it's like while keeping it you know
employer
friendly oh you might be curious to know
a little bit of a background about me my
fate was sealed at the age of four when
while watching Lion King I declared that
I would become an animation artist
unlike most childhood proclamations this
one stuck and I've been to art school
for seven years I got an animation
degree and I got a second degree in
illustration from a British University
at some point I also got to learn from a
former Disney animator as soon as I
could open a bank account I started
freelancing background design inet
character design storyboarding you name
it my first real job was a media company
making YouTube content then for some
unrelated reasons I packed my things and
I moved to
Korea how I manipulated my way into a
Korean Animation Studio upon arrival my
Korean vocabulary was limited to
ordering food and apologizing for my
existence and my first employment was
illustrating books for an English
Academy a GI that I found on Craigslist
but I thought something more substantial
and so naturally I have become an
English teacher in a hagw one it was an
art hagw one so I rationalized it as
adjacent to the
field but the truth was obvious I was
wildly overqualified for the job and my
soul began to wither when my son started
going to the daycare I sent out a flurry
of applications to Animation Studios at
the time my Korean was conversational at
best but I put together a CV with the
help of an AI in Korean signed up on job
career.com and applied to 20 to 30
places I think out of all that I got
four interviews and in the end I had two
offers to choose from I went was the one
that gave me more work from home
flexibility the interviews or uh what
Korean Studios actually care about so
here is the biggest thing language
almost every employer was worried about
my Korean level and one even suggested
that I pay them for the Korean classes
before they would hire me delightful
younger teams stand to speak some
English so if you're not fluent you will
have easier time with companies run by
younger people the older Generations
less so the studio I chose for the newer
one which I love because smaller teams
actually need you you feel like you're
contributing something instead of being
an interchangeable C and during my
interview they told me your Korean level
is fine we will communicate through
drawings a sensible proposition so I was
like yeah okay these are my people and
additionally I would say that they often
require a bachelor degree just because
of the fierce competition in the
industry so let's get it into what it's
like working in the Korean Animation
studio and number
one office culture or the rals of forced
camaraderie in Korea team bonding is not
a suggestion it's a mandate there is
something called quishi it's a sacred
company dinner during Quik you usually
consume an alarming amount of meat and
Soju all in the name of workplace Unity
drinking games are common but fear not
from my experience no one shall pour
alcohol down your throat against your
will they will however expect your
presence when well into the night I do
not drink alcohol well a lot of it so I
just had to sit through endless barbecue
nights which for someone who doesn't
like heavy dinners is a form of
suffering on its own also even though
socializing is huge here actually making
friends at work is harder than you think
allice culture is friendly but once you
clock out everyone's just disappearing
into their own lives number
two overtime the lifestyle Choice
overtime is an unspoken batch of honor
some people stay crazy late come in on
the weekends or even sleep at the office
I have a kid so my contract hours are
sacred the latest I ever state was 7
minutes and I just really wanted to
finish a scene number
three lunchtime lunchtime is a Social
Hour in my studio most of the time we
eat together if you bring your own lunch
and eat alone that's Ain to social Exile
it's not a rule but it's kind of is
number four
Korean animation standards and software
so Korean Studios demand Excellence if
you want in your skills should be top
tier and for those accustomed to ton
boom abandon hope clip Studio paint rain
Supreme here offering tool so powerful
that one could theoretically create an
entire movie without leaving the
software it's a beast respective GRE 3D
models animation tools web tunes and
more do check it out number five
life animators pay and getting a Visa
money talk key animator start around $20
per 25 frames if you're fast you can
make it work but if you're slow good
luck the cool part is that Korean
Studios do sponsor visas for talented
artists so if you dream of working here
keep applying and for those hesitant to
relocate I would suggest consider Japan
they are significantly more inclined to
higher remote Talent
number six is that corre
correct
yeah my studio mainly does outsourcing
so I worked on some pretty cool stuff
including projects
some stuff and a lot of it there are
also projects that I cannot talk about
yet because of the NDA my CEO is an
industry Gant and he has been in
animation for decades I am the most
grateful that someone like this sees
potential and alone for me
so my Odyssey through Korean animation
industry continues it's been challenging
if you're thinking of working here my
advice learn Korean build a killer
portfolio and above all persevere
someone will take a chance on you oh and
before I go when I first started I found
myself a drift in the sea of Korean
animation terminology so in the feat of
both necessity and Desperation I created
a Korean to English animation dictionary
if such a thing interests you do let me
know down below and I may well make a SE
